title: 55-year-old woman with shortness of breath after a flight
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 doctor in the Emergency Department. Mrs Patricia Hennessey, a 55-year-old office manager, presents with 4 hours of sudden onset shortness of breath and left-sided pleuritic chest pain. She returned from a 10-hour flight from Sydney 2 days ago. Please take a history focused on the likely diagnosis and request appropriate investigations.
examinerNotes: This is a pulmonary embolism scenario. Key features: immobility (long-haul flight), pleuritic chest pain, calf swelling (DVT), tachycardia. Wells score should be calculated. CTPA is the investigation of choice. Candidate should anticoagulate promptly.

patient.name: Patricia Hennessey
patient.presentingComplaint: Sudden shortness of breath and sharp chest pain when breathing in
patient.reasonForAttendance: I can't breathe properly and my chest hurts when I take a deep breath

hpi.site: Left-sided chest pain, worse on breathing in
hpi.onset: Sudden, started 4 hours ago at rest
associatedSymptoms: Left calf pain and swelling for 3 days
hpi.timing: Came on suddenly, continuous since then
hpi.relievingFactors: Sitting still, nothing relieves it much
hpi.severity: 7 out of 10 chest pain. Breathless walking a few steps.

socialHistory.travel: Returned from Sydney 2 days ago — 10-hour flight, sat in window seat, minimal mobility

systemsReview.respiratory: SOB and pleuritic chest pain as above
systemsReview.musculoskeletal: Left calf pain and swelling for 3 days

examination.general: Anxious woman, tachypnoeic at rest, using accessory muscles
examination.cardiovascular: Tachycardia. No murmurs. No raised JVP.
examination.respiratory: Reduced air entry left base. No wheeze. Dull to percussion left base.
examination.musculoskeletal: Left calf swollen 3cm greater than right, tender on palpation, erythematous — DVT signs

investigations.name: CT Pulmonary Angiogram
investigations.result: Filling defects in right main pulmonary artery and bilateral segmental pulmonary arteries consistent with pulmonary emboli. Small right-sided pleural effusion.
investigations.interpretation: BILATERAL PULMONARY EMBOLI confirmed. Saddle embolus extending into right main pulmonary artery. Significant clot burden.

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.result: Sinus tachycardia 112 bpm. S1Q3T3 pattern (S wave in I, Q wave and inverted T wave in III). Right axis deviation. No ST elevation.
investigations.interpretation: S1Q3T3 pattern and sinus tachycardia — classic (though non-specific) findings in PE. Sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding.

investigations.name: D-dimer
investigations.interpretation: Markedly elevated D-dimer. Note: high pre-test probability means D-dimer is not needed for diagnosis — CTPA should be performed regardless.

investigations.name: Arterial Blood Gas
investigations.interpretation: Type 1 respiratory failure (hypoxia without hypercapnia). Respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation. Consistent with PE.

patient.emotionalState: Frightened and anxious. Concerned about the pain and inability to breathe. Asking if this could be a blood clot.
patient.communicationStyle: Short of breath so speaks in short sentences. Cooperative and answers directly.
hiddenConcerns: Is this a blood clot from the flight? I heard about this happening
My sister had a clot — am I at higher risk?
I'm meant to fly back to visit family next month
doNotVolunteer: Calf swelling — only mention if asked about leg symptoms
Family history — only if asked about family history of clotting
Oral contraceptive pill history — only if asked about medications
Details of flight — only if asked about recent travel or immobility

dataGathering.criterion: Establishes pleuritic nature of chest pain
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Asks about sharp pain worsened by breathing — key PE feature
Asks about calf pain, swelling, redness

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about recent immobility or travel
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Specifically asks about long-haul flight, prolonged immobility, surgery

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about risk factors for VTE
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: OCP, pregnancy, malignancy, thrombophilia, previous VTE, family history
Orders CT pulmonary angiogram as definitive investigation

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: States pulmonary embolism as primary differential
References Wells criteria or clinical probability assessment
Prescribes LMWH or direct oral anticoagulant (apixaban/rivaroxaban) promptly
Aware of thrombolysis for haemodynamically unstable PE

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Explains diagnosis and management in lay terms
interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Uses 'blood clot' not PE, explains treatment

references.source: ESC Guidelines on acute pulmonary embolism 2019

learningObjectives: Identify risk factors for venous thromboembolism
Apply Wells score for PE clinical probability
Select CTPA as definitive investigation for PE
Initiate appropriate anticoagulation for confirmed PE
Recognise features of massive PE requiring thrombolysis
